example2: The couple seemed mismatched in personality.
example3: The furniture was an odd collection of mismatched pieces.
Explanation: This adjective describes items or people that are put together but do not match in style, size, color, or character. It can be a deliberate style choice (mismatched earrings) or an accident (mismatched socks). It often implies a lack of harmony or coordination. The word is formed from the verb 'mismatch'. While sometimes a problem, in fashion and design, a 'mismatched' look can be intentional and creative.
usageTip: Use 'mismatched' to describe things that are supposed to be a pair or set but are different from each other.
commonMistake: Using 'mismatched' to mean simply 'different'. It implies an unsuccessful or awkward pairing.

meaning: Not fitting or compatible with something else
example: The software version was mismatched with the database requirements.
example2: We found mismatched data fields in the import file.
example3: The hardware cables and ports were mismatched.
Explanation: A 'mismatched' item is one that does not properly connect or work with the other piece it is supposed to be paired with. This is a very common source of errors in IT, such as when your frontend expects an integer but the API sends a string, or when your hardware uses one connector type but your cable uses another. Identifying these mismatches early in the development cycle is critical to ensuring your system components integrate smoothly.
usageTip: Use 'mismatched' when things do not fit together.
commonMistake: Assuming mismatched parts will work; they will almost always cause errors.
